name.text: Week 16 – Q6: Wireless Power Transfer Efficiency Design

questiontext.text:
A wireless power transfer (WPT) system, similar to a Qi phone charger, has the following parameters:

  • Transmitter coil: \(L_1 = {@L1_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(R_1 = {@R1@}\;\Omega\)
  • Receiver coil: \(L_2 = {@L2_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(R_2 = {@R2@}\;\Omega\)
  • Coupling coefficient: \(k = {@k_val@}\)
  • Operating frequency: \(f = {@f_kHz@}\) kHz

Assume resonant matching (reactive parts cancelled).

(a) Calculate the mutual inductance \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\).

\(M = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] H

(b) Calculate the coupling reactance \(\omega M\).

\(\omega M = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]] \(\Omega\)

(c) Calculate the power transfer efficiency (at resonance):

\[\eta = \frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2}\]

\(\eta = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] (as a decimal, e.g. 0.966)

(d) At what minimum frequency \(f_{\min}\) (in kHz) would the efficiency reach at least 95%?

Hint: rearrange the efficiency formula to solve for \(\omega\), then convert to \(f\).

\(f_{\min} = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] kHz

(e) Which course modules are involved in the design of this WPT system?

[[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(L_1 = {@L1_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(L_2 = {@L2_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(R_1 = {@R1@}\;\Omega\), \(R_2 = {@R2@}\;\Omega\), \(k = {@k_val@}\), \(f = {@f_kHz@}\) kHz.

(a) Mutual inductance:

\[M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = {@k_val@} \times \sqrt{{@L1_uH@} \times {@L2_uH@}} \;\mu\text{H} = {@M_uH_disp@}\;\mu\text{H} = {@tans1@} \text{ H}\]

(b) Coupling reactance:

\[\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times {@f_kHz@} \times 10^3 \text{ rad/s}\]

\[\omega M = {@float(omega_val)@} \times {@tans1@} = {@tans2@}\;\Omega\]

(c) Power transfer efficiency:

\[\eta = \frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2} = \frac{({@tans2@})^2}{{@R1@} \times {@R2@} + ({@tans2@})^2} = {@tans3@}\]

This is {@float(tans3*100)@}% efficiency at resonance with ideal alignment.

(d) Minimum frequency for 95% efficiency:

Set \(\eta \geq 0.95\):

\[\frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2} \geq 0.95\]

\[(\omega M)^2 \geq 0.95\,(R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\]

\[0.05\,(\omega M)^2 \geq 0.95\,R_1 R_2\]

\[(\omega M)^2 \geq 19\,R_1 R_2\]

\[\omega \geq \frac{\sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}}{M}\]

\[f_{\min} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\frac{\sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}}{M} = {@tans4@} \text{ kHz}\]

(e) Cross-module connections:

  • M1 (Circuits): Power transfer, equivalent circuit analysis
  • M2 (Laplace): Resonant matching: \(f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})\)
  • M3 (Magnetics): Mutual inductance \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\), Faraday's law for induced EMF

At Qi frequencies (100-200 kHz), \(\lambda \approx 3\) km \(\gg\) coil size, so wave effects (M4) are negligible. WPT uses M1 + M2 + M3.

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("L1=", L1_uH, "uH L2=", L2_uH, "uH R1=", R1, " R2=", R2, " k=", k_val, " f=", f_kHz, "kHz")@}

feedbackvariables.text: err_sum_flag: if abs(M_err_sum) > 0 and abs(ans1 - M_err_sum)/abs(M_err_sum) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You used \(M = k(L_1 + L_2)\), but the correct formula is \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\) (geometric mean, not arithmetic sum).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\). Make sure to convert \(\mu\text{H}\) to H: multiply by \(10^{-6}\).

feedbackvariables.text: err_noomega_flag: if abs(wM_err_noomega) > 0 and abs(ans2 - wM_err_noomega)/abs(wM_err_noomega) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You used \(f \times M\) instead of \(\omega \times M\). Remember: \(\omega = 2\pi f\), not just \(f\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\omega M = 2\pi f \times M\). Convert kHz to Hz: {@f_kHz@} kHz = {@f_Hz@} Hz.

feedbackvariables.text: /* Check if efficiency > 1 (impossible) */ err_over1_flag: if is(ans3 > 1) then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: Your efficiency is greater than 1 (100%), which is physically impossible. Check the formula: \(\eta = (\omega M)^2 / (R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\). This fraction is always less than 1.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\eta = (\omega M)^2 / (R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\). Use your \(\omega M\) from part (b).

truefeedback.text: You solved for \(\omega_{\min}\) correctly but forgot to convert to \(f\): \(f = \omega/(2\pi)\), not \(f = \omega\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Rearrange \(\eta \geq 0.95\): \((\omega M)^2 \geq 19\,R_1 R_2\), so \(\omega \geq \sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}/M\), then \(f = \omega/(2\pi)\). Convert to kHz.

truefeedback.text: Correct. At ~100 kHz, wavelength is ~3 km — far larger than the coil. No wave/radiation effects (M4). WPT uses M1 (circuits), M2 (resonance), and M3 (mutual inductance).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The key question is: does this system need wave analysis (M4)? At {@f_kHz@} kHz, \(\lambda = c/f \approx 3\) km. Since coils are ~cm sized (\(\ll \lambda\)), wave effects are negligible. WPT uses M1 + M2 + M3, not M4.

hint.text: \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\) — note it's the geometric mean \(\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\), not the sum \(L_1 + L_2\). Convert \(\mu\text{H}\) to H by multiplying by \(10^{-6}\).
For part (d): set \(\eta = 0.95\) and solve for \(\omega\). You'll find \((\omega M)^2 = 19\,R_1 R_2\). Then \(\omega = \sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}/M\) and \(f = \omega/(2\pi)\).
For the module classification: compute \(\lambda = c/f\) at the operating frequency. If \(\lambda\) is much larger than the system, wave effects don't matter and M4 is not needed.
